Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â Â ÂThe statistical analysis indicates several key inflection points and periods of oscillation, which highlight the dynamic nature of consumer adoption behaviors. The initial rapid increase and subsequent stabilization provide insights into the critical phases of market penetration and consumer habit formation. The main outcome is the average weekly consumption of each milk type per person. The model compares two variants: a threshold-based approach and a probability-based approach for how agents become disposed to consider their choices. Agents represent adult consumers who make milk choices based on perceived health and environmental characteristics, modified by habit, social influence, and evaluation of previous choices. The model includes 1,000 agents, each part of a social network where influence is a function of interaction probability and agent susceptibility. Social norms are globally perceived and impact the weightings of the choice function. The model monitors the number of agents consuming mostly whole milk or skimmed/semi-skimmed milk and plots the percentage of The mean quantity of skimmed and semi-skimmed milk continues to decrease but at a slower rate, with minor fluctuations. Notable points include slight peaks at steps 19, 27, and 30, where the consumption temporarily increases to around 773.96, 620.98, and 335.18 units respectively. These peaks are followed by subsequent declines, indicating periods of temporary resurgence in whole milk consumption, possibly due to social influences or habit persistence among some agents.
